1	# CLAUDE.md
2	
3	This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
4	
5	## Project Overview
6	
7	APOD Proxy — a Cloudflare Worker that proxies NASA's Astronomy Picture of the Day API behind stable, fixed URLs. Serves today's APOD image (standard or HD) or JSON metadata. Falls back up to 7 days when the daily APOD is a video.
8	
9	## Commands
10	
11	```bash
12	npm run dev            # Start API (localhost:8787) + landing (localhost:8788) in background
13	npm run dev:api        # API dev server only (no landing page)
14	npm run dev:landing    # Landing page Vite dev server only
15	npm run log            # Tail both API and landing page logs (local dev)
16	npm run log:tail       # Show last 20 lines of both logs (local dev)
17	npm run log:api        # Tail API logs only
18	npm run log:api:tail   # Show last 20 lines of API logs
19	npm run log:landing    # Tail landing page logs only
20	npm run log:landing:tail # Show last 20 lines of landing logs
21	npm run log:prod       # Start tailing production Worker logs to file (background)
22	npm run log:prod:tail  # Show last 20 lines of production logs
23	npm run log:prod:watch # Watch production logs live
24	npm run log:prod:stop  # Stop production log tail
25	npm run stop           # Kill dev servers
26	npx tsc --noEmit       # Type-check (no build artifact, Wrangler bundles directly)
27	npm run deploy         # Deploy everything (Worker + landing page)
28	npm run deploy:api     # Deploy Worker only
29	npm run deploy:landing # Build + deploy landing to Cloudflare Pages
30	npm run build:landing  # Vite production build for landing page
31	```
32	
33	## Architecture
34	
35	Single-file Worker (`src/index.ts`) with these routes (plus `.jpg` aliases for each image endpoint):
36	
37	- `GET /` — proxies today's HD APOD image (falls back to standard if unavailable)
38	- `GET /sd` — proxies the standard-res version
39	- `GET /optimized` — optimized image (≤1200px, auto WebP/AVIF via Cloudflare Image Transformations)
40	- `GET /info` — returns JSON metadata
41	- `GET /about` — plain-text description and attribution
42	
43	Caching uses the Workers Cache API (`caches.default`) with a two-tier strategy — no KV, D1, or cron:
44	- **Primary cache** (6h TTL): APOD metadata keyed by ET date, image bytes keyed by NASA URL
45	- **Stale fallback** (24h TTL): same data under a `:stale` suffixed key, served when NASA's API is down or rate-limited
46	
47	Cache keys are date-based in `America/New_York` timezone, so they roll over at midnight ET.
48	
49	The `findLatestImage` function walks backwards from today up to `MAX_LOOKBACK_DAYS` (7) to skip video APODs.
50	
51	The landing page (`landing/`) is built with Vite and SCSS, served at `nasapicture.com` via Cloudflare Pages. Source is `landing/index.html` with styles in `landing/src/styles/` (8 SCSS partials). The Worker serves `api.nasapicture.com` and `get.nasapicture.com`.
52	
53	## Environment
54	
55	- **`NASA_API_KEY`** — optional secret; falls back to `DEMO_KEY` (30 req/hr, sufficient behind cache). Set via `npx wrangler secret put NASA_API_KEY`.
56	- Local secrets go in `.dev.vars` (gitignored).
57	
58	## Design Context
59	
60	### Users
61	Everyone — developers integrating the API into dashboards and side projects, tinkerers building for fun, and general visitors who stumble on the domain out of curiosity about space. The landing page should be immediately understandable to non-technical visitors while giving developers everything they need.
62	
63	### Brand Personality
64	**Clever, minimal, precise.** The voice is confident and concise — says a lot with few words. Engineering craft without jargon. Personality comes through in small details (the /about ASCII art, "That's it. That's the whole integration."), never through excess.
65	
66	### Emotional Goals
67	**Awe + simplicity.** Visitors should feel wonder at the cosmos (the APOD image does the heavy lifting) and relief at how easy the tool is. The design should get out of the way of the content while still feeling crafted.
68	
69	### Aesthetic Direction
70	- **Theme**: Dark space — deep void (#05070c) with atmospheric depth, not flat black
71	- **Accent**: Warm amber/gold (#e8a838) — planned but not yet implemented; current palette is monochrome silver with NASA blue
72	- **Typography**: Source Serif 4 Display (headings), Source Sans 3 (body), Source Code Pro (code) — Adobe Typekit, no generic fonts
73	- **Layout**: Generous whitespace, content-forward, max-width constrained (~5xl)
74	- **Motion**: Subtle and purposeful — twinkling stars, staggered fade-ins, gentle hover lifts. Always respect `prefers-reduced-motion`
75	- **Anti-references**: No AI slop aesthetics, no purple gradients on white, no cookie-cutter SaaS layouts
76	
77	### Design Principles
78	1. **The image is the hero** — everything else supports it. Never compete with the APOD for attention.
79	2. **Earn trust through restraint** — fewer elements, better. If it doesn't serve the visitor, remove it.
80	3. **Craft in the details** — personality lives in small moments (ASCII art, code comments, hover states), not in loud design choices.
81	4. **Accessible by default** — WCAG AA contrast, semantic HTML, reduced-motion alternatives. Accessibility is not optional.
82	5. **Zero friction** — the landing page mirrors the API philosophy: simple to deploy, simple to understand.
83	
84	## Project Board
85	
86	This project uses a structured board system for project management.
87	
88	- **Ask before creating new board files** — don't auto-create tasks, ADRs, etc.
89	- Follow the board system spec for all documentation artifacts
90	- Use `/board:board-show` to see current board state
91	- Use `/board:board-add` to create new items
92	- Use `/board:board-update` after completing significant work to reconcile the board
93	- Use `/board:board-check` when starting work to find related board context
94
```
</details></tool-use>

_**User (2026-04-05 05:07:48Z)**_

in package.json is there a way to do a variable for ports?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-05 05:08:01Z)**_



Not natively in npm scripts. `package.json` doesn't support variables in the `scripts` block.

You could use a `.env`-style approach with something like `cross-env` or `dotenv-cli`, but that adds dependencies for something simple. Another option is a small shell script that sources a config file, but that breaks the nice `npm run` convention.

Honestly, 8787 and 8788 only appear in `dev`, `status`, and `stop`. It's manageable as-is. If the ports ever change, it's a quick find-and-replace in one file.

---

_**User (2026-04-05 05:08:47Z)**_

cant `npm run` run shell files? at what point does it make sense to abstract out to shell scripts

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-05 05:09:01Z)**_



Yes, npm scripts can run shell files (`"dev": "./scripts/dev.sh"`). The tradeoff:

**Stay in package.json when:**
- One-liners or simple chains
- The command is self-documenting at a glance

**Move to shell scripts when:**
- The command is hard to read on one line
- You need variables, conditionals, or loops
- Multiple scripts share config (like port numbers)

Looking at our `dev` and `status` scripts, they're already pushing the readability limit. `dev` especially is a wall of text. A `scripts/` directory with small shell files would let us define ports once and keep things readable.

Want me to refactor into shell scripts?

---

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
source "$(dirname "$0")/config.sh"

MODE="${1:-watch}"    # watch | tail
TARGET="${2:-all}"    # all | api | landing | prod

case "$TARGET" in
  api)     files="$API_LOG" ;;
  landing) files="$LANDING_LOG" ;;
  prod)    files="$PROD_LOG" ;;
  all)     files="$API_LOG $LANDING_LOG" ;;
  *)       echo "Unknown target: $TARGET"; exit 1 ;;
esac

case "$MODE" in
  watch) tail -f $files ;;
  tail)  tail -20 $files ;;
  *)     echo "Unknown mode: $MODE"; exit 1 ;;
esac

```

```text
File created successfully at: /Users/grant/code/nasa-apod/scripts/log.sh
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
source "$(dirname "$0")/config.sh"

ACTION="${1:-start}"  # start | stop

case "$ACTION" in
  start)
    mkdir -p "$LOG_DIR"
    wrangler tail --format pretty > "$PROD_LOG" 2>&1 &
    echo "Production log started → $PROD_LOG"
    ;;
  stop)
    pkill -f 'wrangler tail' 2>/dev/null \
      && echo "Production log stopped" \
      || echo "No production log running"
    ;;
  *) echo "Unknown action: $ACTION"; exit 1 ;;
esac

```

```text
1	---
2	type: task
3	status: todo
4	created: 2026-04-01
5	parent: EPIC-0003
6	blocked-by: TASK-0010
7	---
8	
9	# Add R2 storage layer for APOD image archiving
10	
11	Add Cloudflare R2 as a persistent storage/origin layer between edge cache and NASA API. Every image fetched from NASA gets stored in R2, building an automatic archive and providing resilience if NASA is slow or down.
12	
13	## Infrastructure
14	
15	- Create R2 bucket (e.g., `apod-archive`)
16	- Add R2 binding in `wrangler.toml`: `[[r2_buckets]]` with `binding = "APOD_ARCHIVE"`
17	- Update `Env` interface in `src/index.ts` to include the R2 binding
18	
19	## R2 key structure
20	
21	- `{date}/hd.{ext}` — HD image
22	- `{date}/sd.{ext}` — SD image
23	- `{date}/info.json` — metadata snapshot
24	
25	## Worker changes (`src/index.ts`)
26	
27	- Refactor image fetching to layered lookup: Edge cache → R2 → NASA
28	- On R2 miss: fetch from NASA, store in R2 (`bucket.put()`), then serve
29	- On R2 hit: serve directly from R2 with appropriate `Content-Type`
30	- `/optimized` applies `cf.image` transforms on top of R2-served images
31	- Cache metadata in R2 alongside images
32	
33	## Edge caching preserved
34	
35	- `cf.cacheTtl` and `cf.cacheEverything` remain on all responses
36	- R2 replaces NASA as the origin; edge cache sits in front
37	
38	## Cache invalidation
39	
40	Once an image is in R2, it's permanent — if NASA corrects or replaces an image at the same URL, we'd serve the stale version. Low risk (NASA rarely edits published APODs) but worth a safety valve:
41	
42	- For the current day's image, treat R2 as a cache with a short TTL (e.g., re-fetch from NASA if R2 object is <1 hour old and edge cache misses) — allows corrections to propagate same-day
43	- For past dates, serve from R2 unconditionally (immutable after rollover)
44	- Consider an auth-protected manual purge endpoint (e.g., `DELETE /admin/purge/{date}`) to force re-fetch from NASA on demand
45	
46	## Verification
47	
48	- First request stores to R2 (check via `wrangler r2 object get`)
49	- Subsequent requests serve from R2 without hitting NASA
50	- Edge caching still works in front of R2
51	- `/optimized` transforms apply correctly on R2-sourced images
52
```
</details></tool-use>

7	# Auto-refresh landing page on APOD rollover

9	If a user leaves the landing page open, the image and caption should update automatically when NASA publishes the new APOD — no manual reload required.

17	- Should only poll when the tab is visible (`document.visibilityState`) to avoid wasting requests in background tabs

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-05 05:59:14Z)**_

Here's the plan:

## Auto-refresh on APOD rollover

**Approach:** Poll `/info` every 15 minutes. When the `date` field changes from what's currently displayed, swap the image and caption with a crossfade. Only poll when the tab is visible.

### Implementation (all in `landing/index.html` `<script>`)

**1. Track current date**

Store the displayed date in a variable after the initial fetch:

```js
let currentDate = null;
```

Set it when the initial `/info` response comes back (where we already set the caption).

**2. Add an `updateApod(data)` function**

Extract the existing caption/image/thumbnail update logic into a reusable function. On initial load and on rollover, this function:
- Updates caption title, date, datetime attribute
- Sets `wrapper.style.aspectRatio` from new dimensions
- Fetches new `/thumb`, sets as background
- Fades out the current image (`img.classList.remove('loaded')`)
- Sets new `img.src` to `/optimized` (triggers reload)
- On `img.load`, fades back in

**3. Polling loop**

```js
const POLL_INTERVAL = 15 * 60 * 1000; // 15 minutes

function startPolling() {
  setInterval(async () => {
    if (document.visibilityState !== 'visible') return;
    try {
      const data = await fetch(`${API_BASE}/info`).then(r => r.json());
      if (data.date && data.date !== currentDate) {
        updateApod(data);
      }
    } catch {}
  }, POLL_INTERVAL);
}
```

**4. Visibility handling**

The `setInterval` callback already checks `visibilityState`, so it skips polls when the tab is backgrounded. When the user returns to the tab after a long absence, the next interval tick catches the rollover.

Optionally, also check on `visibilitychange` event for immediate catch-up when returning to the tab:

```js
document.addEventListener('visibilitychange', () => {
  if (document.visibilityState === 'visible') {
    // check immediately
  }
});
```

**5. Transition**

On rollover: fade out image (remove `.loaded` class), swap `src`, fade back in on load. The existing CSS transition handles this. For `prefers-reduced-motion`, skip the fade as we already do.

### What changes

- `landing/index.html` — refactor initial load into `updateApod()`, add polling logic (~20 lines net new)
- No Worker changes needed
- No new dependencies

### What doesn't change

- Poll hits the cached Worker endpoint, so no additional NASA API load
- No WebSocket or SSE needed for this frequency

Want me to implement it?

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-05 06:05:14Z)**_



Good point. Instead of polling, we can calculate exactly when the next rollover happens and set a single `setTimeout`.

**Options:**

1. **Calculated timeout** — Compute milliseconds until midnight ET from the user's local clock, set one `setTimeout` to refresh. After refresh, schedule the next one. Zero unnecessary requests.

2. **Polling `/info`** (original plan) — Simple but wasteful. 96 requests/day if the tab stays open.

3. **Hybrid** — `setTimeout` for midnight ET, then poll briefly (every 2-3 min for ~30 min) since NASA's publish time varies. Stops polling once a new date is detected.

Option 3 is probably the most robust. The exact midnight timeout gets us close, then short-burst polling handles NASA's variable publish delay. Total requests: ~10-15 per rollover instead of 96/day.

Though honestly, option 1 might be good enough. If someone has the tab open at midnight ET, a single `/info` fetch at that moment will either get the new image or the cached old one (which will naturally update within the cache TTL). The 6-hour cache TTL means worst case they'd see the new image by 6am ET anyway, but in practice the cache rolls over at midnight ET since the keys are date-based.

Actually, since our cache keys are date-based and roll at midnight ET, a fresh `/info` request after midnight ET will be a cache miss for the new date and fetch fresh data from NASA. So option 1 (single timeout at midnight ET) should work cleanly.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-05 06:12:17Z)**_



Yes, it's a full HTTP timestamp like `Fri, 04 Apr 2026 12:34:56 GMT`. Every HTTP response includes it automatically.

So we can compute time until midnight ET from the `Date` header of the `/info` response we already make on page load. No extra requests, no dependency on the user's clock.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-05 06:20:45Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-05 06:20:52Z)**_

A few things worth noting:

1. **Browser image cache** — On rollover, `img.src` is still `/optimized`. The browser may serve the cached old image. Need to bust the cache with a query param like `?d=2026-04-05` using the new date.

2. **Long setTimeout drift** — `setTimeout` for hours isn't precise. Browsers throttle timers in background tabs. The polling phase covers this, but the initial timeout could fire late.

3. **Tab hidden for days** — If someone returns after multiple days, the `setTimeout` already fired (and polling capped out). The `visibilitychange` handler should do an immediate `/info` check when the tab becomes visible again to catch this.

4. **Focus mode** — If the user is in focus mode (dimmed UI, viewing the image), should the image swap underneath them? Could be jarring. Might want to defer the swap until they exit focus mode.

5. **Multiple rollovers** — After each successful rollover, need to re-schedule the next midnight timeout. Not just a one-time setup.

The browser cache one (#1) is the most critical, the rest are edge cases. Want me to add these to the NOTE?

1	---
2	type: note
3	status: inbox
4	created: 2026-03-31
5	---
6	
7	# Auto-refresh landing page on APOD rollover
8	
9	If a user leaves the landing page open, the image and caption should update automatically when NASA publishes the new APOD — no manual reload required.
10	
11	## Approach: Scheduled timeout + short-burst polling
12	
13	Rather than polling all day, calculate a `setTimeout` to midnight ET from the user's local clock. Once midnight ET arrives, poll `/info` every 2 minutes until `data.date` changes from what's currently displayed (NASA's publish time varies by a few minutes). Cap polling at 30 minutes, then stop and schedule the next midnight.
14	
15	This results in ~15 requests per rollover and zero the rest of the day.
16	
17	### Sequence
18	
19	1. On page load, store `currentDate` from initial `/info` response
20	2. Compute ms until next midnight ET, set `setTimeout`
21	3. At midnight ET, start polling `/info` every 2 min
22	4. When `data.date !== currentDate`, call `updateApod(data)` and stop polling
23	5. After 30 min with no change, stop polling and schedule next midnight
24	6. Skip polls when `document.visibilityState !== 'visible'`, check immediately on `visibilitychange`
25	
26	### Image transition on rollover
27	
28	- Fade out current image (remove `.loaded` class)
29	- Fetch new `/thumb`, set as wrapper background
30	- Set new `img.src` to `/optimized` (triggers reload)
31	- Update aspect ratio from new dimensions
32	- On `img.load`, fade back in
33	- Respect `prefers-reduced-motion` (skip fade, show immediately)
34	
35	### Time source: CF `Date` header (preferred)
36	
37	Use the `Date` response header from the initial `/info` fetch as the authoritative time source. This comes from Cloudflare's edge server (NTP-synced), so it's reliable regardless of the user's local clock. Parse it to compute ms until midnight ET, no extra requests needed.
38	
39	```js
40	const res = await fetch(`${API_BASE}/info`);
41	const serverNow = new Date(res.headers.get('Date'));
42	// compute ms until midnight ET from serverNow
43	```
44	
45	### Alternatives considered
46	
47	**User's local clock** — Could use `new Date()` from the user's machine to compute time until midnight ET. Simpler, but assumes the user's clock is accurate. Rejected because clock skew on user machines is common enough to cause missed or premature rollovers.
48	
49	**External time APIs** — worldtimeapi.org and timeapi.io are free, no-key options. Unnecessary here since we already get authoritative time from the CF `Date` header, but available if we ever need a dedicated time source.
50	
51	### Implementation notes
52	
53	- Refactor initial caption/image/thumbnail setup into a reusable `updateApod(data)` function
54	- All changes in `landing/index.html` script block, no Worker changes needed
55	- Cache keys are date-based and roll at midnight ET, so post-midnight `/info` requests will cache-miss for the new date and fetch fresh from NASA
56	- Zero new dependencies
57
```
</details></tool-use>

_**User (2026-04-05 06:38:31Z)**_

is there case for SSE here?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-05 06:38:46Z)**_



The Worker would need to hold open a connection per client and push a message once per day. For a single daily event, that's a lot of idle connection time for very little payoff.

SSE makes sense when updates are frequent or unpredictable. Here we know exactly when the rollover happens (midnight ET, give or take a few minutes), so the client-side countdown + burst poll is simpler, cheaper, and doesn't require any Worker changes.

SSE would also mean rethinking the Worker architecture since Cloudflare Workers have CPU time limits per request, though you could work around that with Durable Objects. But that's a lot of complexity for "check once a day."

The client-side approach is the right call here.

@@ -8,10 +8,72 @@ created: 2026-03-31
 
 If a user leaves the landing page open, the image and caption should update automatically when NASA publishes the new APOD — no manual reload required.
 
-## Considerations
+## Approach: Self-correcting countdown + short-burst polling
 
-- NASA publishes around midnight Eastern, but the exact time varies (sometimes delayed)
-- Polling `/info` periodically and comparing the date to what's currently displayed is the simplest approach
-- A reasonable interval might be every 15–30 minutes — frequent enough to catch the rollover without hammering the API (which is cached on the Worker side anyway)
-- When a new date is detected: update the image `src`, caption title, and date, with a fade transition
-- Should only poll when the tab is visible (`document.visibilityState`) to avoid wasting requests in background tabs
+Two phases: a coarse countdown that converges on midnight ET, then burst polling to catch the exact publish moment.
+
+### Phase 1: Countdown
+
+On page load, compute the offset between the user's clock and CF server time (from the `/info` response `Date` header). Use this offset to calculate a corrected "time until midnight ET." Run a `setInterval` every 5 minutes that recalculates the remaining time. This self-corrects for:
+- Long `setTimeout` drift (browsers throttle timers in background tabs)
+- Tabs hidden for hours or days (each tick recalculates from scratch)
+- No single long timeout that fires late or not at all
+
+When the countdown shows <5 minutes to midnight ET, transition to phase 2.
+
+### Phase 2: Burst polling
+
+Poll `/info` every 2 minutes, comparing `data.date` to `currentDate`. When the date changes, call `updateApod(data)` and stop polling. Cap at 30 minutes (15 requests max), then re-enter phase 1 and schedule the next midnight.
+
+Total requests per rollover: ~15. Total requests the rest of the day: zero.
+
+### Sequence
+
+1. On page load, store `currentDate` from initial `/info` response
+2. Compute clock offset from CF `Date` header: `serverTime - localTime`
+3. Start 5-min `setInterval` that recalculates ms until midnight ET using the offset
+4. When <5 min remain, clear the interval and start 2-min burst polling
+5. When `data.date !== currentDate`, call `updateApod(data)`, stop polling, re-enter step 3 for next day
+6. After 30 min with no date change, stop polling and re-enter step 3
+7. On `visibilitychange` to visible, do an immediate `/info` check (catches long-hidden tabs)
+
+### Image transition on rollover
+
+- Fade out current image (remove `.loaded` class)
+- Fetch new `/thumb`, set as wrapper background
+- Cache-bust the image by appending the new date: `img.src = \`${API_BASE}/optimized?d=${data.date}\``
+- Update aspect ratio from new dimensions
+- On `img.load`, fade back in
+- Respect `prefers-reduced-motion` (skip fade, show immediately)
+- Swap happens regardless of focus mode
+
+### Time source: CF `Date` header (preferred)
+
+Use the `Date` response header from the initial `/info` fetch as the authoritative time source. This comes from Cloudflare's edge server (NTP-synced), so it's reliable regardless of the user's local clock. Parse it to compute ms until midnight ET, no extra requests needed.
+
+```js
+const res = await fetch(`${API_BASE}/info`);
+const serverNow = new Date(res.headers.get('Date'));
+// compute ms until midnight ET from serverNow
+```
+
+### Alternatives considered
+
+**User's local clock** — Could use `new Date()` from the user's machine to compute time until midnight ET. Simpler, but assumes the user's clock is accurate. Rejected because clock skew on user machines is common enough to cause missed or premature rollovers.
+
+**External time APIs** — worldtimeapi.org and timeapi.io are free, no-key options. Unnecessary here since we already get authoritative time from the CF `Date` header, but available if we ever need a dedicated time source.
+
+### Edge cases
+
+- **Browser image cache** — The `/optimized` URL doesn't change day-to-day, so the browser may serve a stale cached image on rollover. Bust it with a date query param: `/optimized?d=2026-04-05`. The Worker ignores query params for routing but the browser treats it as a new URL.
+- **Multiple rollovers** — After each successful rollover, re-enter the countdown phase for the next midnight. Not a one-time setup.
+- **Tab hidden for days** — The `visibilitychange` handler does an immediate `/info` check, catching any missed rollovers regardless of how long the tab was hidden.
+- **Thumb cache busting** — Same browser cache issue as `/optimized` applies to `/thumb`. Append `?d=${data.date}` to the thumb fetch on rollover too.
+- **Object URL cleanup** — Each rollover creates a new blob URL for the thumbnail via `URL.createObjectURL()`. Revoke the previous one with `URL.revokeObjectURL()` to avoid memory leaks.
+
+### Implementation notes
+
+- Refactor initial caption/image/thumbnail setup into a reusable `updateApod(data)` function
+- All changes in `landing/index.html` script block, no Worker changes needed
+- Cache keys are date-based and roll at midnight ET, so post-midnight `/info` requests will cache-miss for the new date and fetch fresh from NASA
+- Zero new dependencies
